coredata.dc:title: Biomass spinach-drived metal-free carbon dots-based nanozyme for multimodal nitrite sensing and functionalized by glucose oxidase as ROS amplifiers to enhance tumor therapy

coredata.dc:description: The metal-free carbon dots (CDs) nanozyme, which is endowed generation of multiple reactive oxygen species (ROS), followed by highly selective chemical sensing, remains a critical challenge. The exceptional biocatalytic properties of glucose oxidase (GOx) have spurred the development of GOx-functionalized nanocatalysts for cancer therapy. Here, the innovative free metal-doped CDs and CDs@GOx nanozymes with peroxidase (POD)-like activity were developed, which specifically catalyzed H₂O₂ to engender multiple ROS including •O2 −, 1O2 and •OH, to oxidize colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue ox-TMB, indicating both nanozymes can be as ROS amplifiers to enhance tumor therapy. The introduction of NO₂− triggered a distinct color change from blue to green ascribed to the diazotization of ox-TMB along with quenching the fluorescence of CDs, which endowed high selectivity and sensitivity for NO2 − detection. Furthermore, CDs catalyzed endogenous H₂O₂ within tumor cells, to effectively destroy cancer cells rather than normal cells. As expected, CDs@GOx preferentially catalyze glucose in cancer cells to further supply H2O2, allowing more ROS accumulation, thereby realizing the integration of starvation therapy and ROS therapy of cancer. Notably, in vivo anti-tumor efficacy demonstrated that CDs and CDs@GOx markedly inhibited tumor growth without external stimulation with neglected side effects. Compared to the saline group, the tumor size was reduced by 3 or 4 times for CDs and CDs@GOx, respectively. This metal-free CDs tailors a convenient and impactful nanoplatform for chemical sensing and as ROS amplifiers to enhance tumor therapy by non-invasive treatment.

head.ce:title: Biomass spinach-drived metal-free carbon dots-based nanozyme for multimodal nitrite sensing and functionalized by glucose oxidase as ROS amplifiers to enhance tumor therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The metal-free carbon dots (CDs) nanozyme, which is endowed generation of multiple reactive oxygen species (ROS), followed by highly selective chemical sensing, remains a critical challenge. The exceptional biocatalytic properties of glucose oxidase (GOx) have spurred the development of GOx-functionalized nanocatalysts for cancer therapy. Here, the innovative free metal-doped CDs and CDs@GOx nanozymes with peroxidase (POD)-like activity were developed, which specifically catalyzed H₂O₂ to engender multiple ROS including •O2 −, 1O2 and •OH, to oxidize colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue ox-TMB, indicating both nanozymes can be as ROS amplifiers to enhance tumor therapy. The introduction of NO₂− triggered a distinct color change from blue to green ascribed to the diazotization of ox-TMB along with quenching the fluorescence of CDs, which endowed high selectivity and sensitivity for NO2 − detection. Furthermore, CDs catalyzed endogenous H₂O₂ within tumor cells, to effectively destroy cancer cells rather than normal cells. As expected, CDs@GOx preferentially catalyze glucose in cancer cells to further supply H2O2, allowing more ROS accumulation, thereby realizing the integration of starvation therapy and ROS therapy of cancer. Notably, in vivo anti-tumor efficacy demonstrated that CDs and CDs@GOx markedly inhibited tumor growth without external stimulation with neglected side effects. Compared to the saline group, the tumor size was reduced by 3 or 4 times for CDs and CDs@GOx, respectively. This metal-free CDs tailors a convenient and impactful nanoplatform for chemical sensing and as ROS amplifiers to enhance tumor therapy by non-invasive treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are highly reactive molecules derived from oxygen, including peroxides, superoxide, hydroxyl radicals, etc. [1]. The role of ROS in biological systems is multifaceted, including signal transduction, cell protection, growth and development regulation [1]. However, under various factors such as ultraviolet radiation, heat exposure, and inflammation, ROS levels sharply increase, which may lead to cell damage. Fortunately, biological systems have developed mechanisms for clearing ROS to maintain ROS balance and protect cells from damage. Therefore, the redox balance of biological organisms is crucial for maintaining the stability of the intracellular environment. Nevertheless, when this balance is disrupted, it may lead to abnormal cellular function and subsequently trigger diseases, such as cancer. Researches have shown that the growth and spread of cancer cells are closely related to the imbalance of redox homeostasis [2,3]. The increased ROS levels are thought to have a major role in tumor development and progression. It is well known that during the occurrence and development of cancer, tumor microenvironment (TME) is accompanied by a series of abnormal features, including hypoxia, low pH, overexpression of glutathione (GSH), glucose and H2O2, etc. As such, regulating cellular redox homeostasis by elevating ROS levels is considered a promising cancer treatment strategy [1]. As far as we know, new methods for treating cancer by generating ROS mainly include photodynamic therapy (PDT) and chemodynamic therapy (CDT). For PDT, a treatment method that treats cancer by irradiating photosensitizers with a specific laser to generate ROS, it possesses advantages of non-invasiveness and spatiotemporal controllability [4]. However, both hypoxia of cancer cells and limited light penetration depth severely restrict the development of PDT [5]. Apart from this, an accurate tumor localization imaging guidance is also necessary to implement external light source stimulation for PDT. For CDT, it is emerging as a new ROS-mediated cancer treatment strategy, which utilizes chemodynamic agents to catalyze the generation of highly cytotoxic ROS from overexpressed H2O2 in the TME through Fenton or Fenton-like reactions, inducing tumor cell apoptosis or other forms of cell death [6]. Unfortunately, almost all CDT require supplement of external H2O2 to induce ROS production, and insufficient endogenous H2O2 in vivo severely limits the efficacy of CDT [6]. Additionally, most CDT involves the metal ions (eg. Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+), and their inevitable toxic side effects further hinder their applications in the biological field. Therefore, developing novel ROS-mediated cancer treatment strategy remains a huge challenge.

Nanozyme, a novel type of functional nanomaterial exhibiting enzyme-like activity, has emerged and garnered extensive research attention [7]. In comparison with natural enzymes, nanozymes possess merits of high catalytic efficiency, stable activity, low cost, and convenient preparation [8–10], making them widely applied in sensing, food safety, environmental protection, and biomedicine [11–13]. The most common applications of nanozymes encompass two main aspects: one is that they are employed in constructing colorimetric probes for detecting a wide range of substances (including glucose, environmental pollutants, antibiotics, etc.) by utilizing the reaction between catalytic products and chromogenic agents [14,15]; the other is that various types of nanozymes have the ability to modulate intracellular redox levels by interacting with substrates, consequently influencing ROS and impacting cell fate, such as apoptosis and autophagy [16,17]. Various materials have been investigated as nanozymes due to their diversity and universality, such as fullerene, polymers, cyclodextrin, and metal-based materials [18–21]. Despite their potential, these nanozymes suffer from drawbacks like complex synthesis, high production costs, and poor biocompatibility [22]. Hence, there is an urgent need to design nanomaterials with easy preparation methods, enzyme-like activity, and high biosafety.

Among numerous nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) with a size <10 nm have abundant dangling bonds (−OH, −C=O and −NH2) and high specific surface area, which are beneficial for exposing active sites [23,24]. In addition, CDs possess excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity, and have been used to construct nanozymes for sensing and biomedical fields [25–28]. Glucose oxidase (GOx), an oxidoreductase, specifically and efficiently catalyzes the oxidation of glucose to produce gluconic acid and H2O2. Its significant potential has led to its increasing application in biomedical tumor synergistic therapy [29]. For example, Wu et al. [30] reported a CDs nanozyme with oxidase-like activity for rapid and accurate analysis of ethyl carbamate through dual modes of colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence. Luo et al. [31] designed a bifunctional CDs as a photosensitizer and catalase-like nanozyme functionalized by cascade GOx to address hypoxia and enhance PDT with the help of external laser irradiation. However, few studies have combined the enzymatic catalytic properties of CDs with their inherent fluorescence features to achieve multi-modal sensing of analytes. Besides, most of CDs-based nanozymes are metal mediated, the unsatisfactory catalytic activity and inevitable toxic side effects of metals further limit their applications in biological field [32,33]. Therefore, it is necessary to explore innovative and multifunctional metal-free CDs-based nanozymes with ideal activity and biosafety for multi-mode sensing and biomedicine.

Nitrite (NO2 −), as a common food color protectant and preservative, has been widely used in catering industry. However, excessive NO2 − in the body poses a serious threat to human health [34]. Fluorescence and colorimetric methods are considered the most promising portable NO2 − detection due to their advantages of fast reaction and simple operation [35,36]. Nevertheless, most of designed optical sensors currently can only provide single performance either colorimetry or fluorescence, making it difficult to eliminate external interference and the application scenarios are strictly limited [37,38]. Hence, multi-mode sensing strategy is considered a more promising approach. On the one hand, multi-modal methods can provide optional and suitable modes to achieve objects detection to meet the requirements of complex environmental. On the other hand, the results obtained from different modes can be mutually verified, effectively improving the accuracy and reliability of object detection to avoid false outputs. In addition, most reports for detecting NO2 − in food adopted the China National Standard (GB 5009.33–2016) for sample pretreatment, which is cumbersome to operate. Therefore, it is essential to design a simple operation and reasonable sensing method to achieve multi-mode detection of NO2 −.

With the above considerations, herein, spinach powder as a precursor prepared novel CDs with good optical properties and excellent stability by a facile one-step solvothermal method (Scheme 1a). The CDs exhibit excellent peroxidase (POD)-like activity and can catalyze the H2O2 to produce various ROS, including •OH, •O2 − and 1O2, oxidizing colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxidized-TMB (ox-TMB) (Scheme 1b). When NO2 − is introduced, the generated ox-TMB reacts specifically with NO2 − to produce diazotized ox-TMB, causing the color to change from blue to green. Meanwhile, diazotized ox-TMB significantly quenched the fluorescence of CDs through internal filtration effect. By utilizing the colorimetric and fluorescence signal changes caused by NO2 −, combined with smartphone color recognition application, the multi-modal, fast, convenient, and reliable detection of NO2 − has been achieved (Scheme 1c), which has improved detection efficiency compared to the China National Standard (GB 5009.33–2016). Meanwhile, due to their excellent catalytic activity, CDs have also been used as ROS agents to effectively eliminate cancer cells by catalyzing in situ the excess H2O2 in cancer cells to produce highly toxic ROS rather than in normal cells. To maximize the efficacy of biomaterials, GOx is covalently attached to CDs to achieve CDs@GOx. The CDs@GOx can effectively decompose the energy of tumor cells and further provide H2O2 for the production of more ROS, thereby realizing the combination therapy of starvation therapy and ROS treatment, and further enhancing the therapeutic effect on in vivo tumor treatment without metal ions-doped or external stimuli (Scheme 1d).
The detailed experimental steps were mentioned in supplementary material.

The morphology and structure of CDs were firstly investigated. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1A) confirmed that CDs was successfully prepared with an average diameter of 4.08 ± 0.43 nm. The hydrodynamic diameter of CDs obtained using dynamic light scattering (DLS) was 35.5 nm (Fig. S1A) with good stability in 72 h (Fig. S1B). The significant differences between the size of CDs obtained by TEM and DLS may belong to the formation of hydration layers on their surfaces or the weak aggregation caused by surface functional groups [39]. Then, the surface functional groups of CDs were investigated by fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (The red line in Fig. 1B). The peaks at around 3420, 2935, 2858, 1604, 1397, 1171 and 1001 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of O−H/N−H, C−H, S−H, CO, C−O, C−N and C−S, respectively, and the peaks located at 1604, 786 and 617 cm−1 were belonged to the bending vibration of N−H, =C−H and O−H, indicating that the surface of CDs contains various hydrophilic groups such as amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and sulfhydryl groups. Meanwhile, the X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) (Fig. 1C) displayed four peaks at 165.4, 281.2, 398.3 and 529.0 eV, which uncovered the main elements in CDs are S, C, N and O. The high-resolved C1s spectrum (Fig. S2A) contained three noticeable peaks: 284.9 eV for CC, 286.4 eV for C−O and 288.7 eV for O=C−O. N1s spectrum (Fig. S2B) exhibited two peaks at 400.2 eV and 401.7 eV, corresponding to the presence of C−N and N−H.

O1s spectrum (Fig. S2C) was separated into two distinct peaks, matching with CO (531.6 eV) and C−OH (532.8 eV). S2p spectrum displayed the obvious peaks of −C−SO3−C− and −C−SO4−C− at 168.6 eV and 169.9 eV, respectively (Fig. S2D). Moreover, the zeta potential in Fig. 1D of CDs is −5.16 mV, speculating that the negatively charged groups (−COOH, −OH) may be exposed to the surface of CDs.

Next, optical characteristics were investigated. The obvious absorption of CDs at 203 nm and 285 nm were attributed to π–π* transition of CC in the carbon core in Fig. 1E. As shown in Fig. S3A, the position of the emission peak and the fluorescence intensity were changed correspondingly with the excitation wavelength increases from 280 nm to 500 nm. This phenomenon thanks to excitation-dependent fluorescence properties, which are typical of fluorescent nanomaterials [40]. The fluorescence spectra of CDs display an excitation and emission maximum at 360 and 460 nm, respectively, and shows the light yellow under daylight (left) and blue fluorescence under UV light (right) (Fig. S3B). Moreover, the quantum yield (QY) of CDs was calculated to be 8.82 % using quinine sulfate (QY with 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4 at 360 nm excitation) as a reference (Fig. S4). Importantly, the fluorescence intensity of CDs shows neglected changes at continuous fluorescence scanning for 30 min, high salt concentrations, wide pH values of 2–11 (Fig. S5AC), and high concentration of ions or small molecules (Fig. S5DF), proving its high stability in biological environments, which endows them a great application prospect in biological field.

The CDs were then coated with GOx (52 μg/mg) for cascading nanozyme construction, where the GOx can efficiently breakdown energy nutrients of tumor cells and provide H2O2 for the CDs to further enhanced ROS level for tumor treatment. The TEM image of CDs@GOx nanocatalysts revealed spherical morphology as shown in Fig. 1F, suggesting that the coupling of GOx had no effect on the geometric morphology. However, the average size and hydrodynamic diameter of CDs@GOx were determined to be 13.45 ± 1.69 nm and 158.8 nm (Fig. S1C), respectively, and they are larger than that of pristine CDs and also remained stable within 72 h (Fig. S1D). More importantly, CDs@GOx exhibits positive charge (3.99 mV) in Fig. 1D, supporting effective GOx loading. In contrast to the FTIR spectrum of CDs, the 2985 cm−1 band of CDs@GOx is the characteristic peak of stretching vibration of saturated C−H bond in GOx and the peak at 1280 cm−1 is the deformation vibration of C−H in GOx in Fig. 1B, which clarifying the successful preparation of CDs@GOx. By comparing the optical properties of CDs@GOx with CDs, it was found that the UV–vis absorption peak of CDs@GOx was blue shifted from 285 nm to 263 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs@GOx at 460 nm was weakened than that of CDs (Fig. 1E), implying that the existence of GOx in CDs@GOx. Moreover, CDs@GOx also exhibit high fluorescence stability under continuous fluorescence scanning for 30 min, high salt concentration, and a wide pH range of 2–11 (Fig. S5AC).

As we all know, POD can catalyze H₂O₂ to oxidize TMB to ox-TMB, turning the solution from colorless to blue and generating a characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm [41,42]. Therefore, TMB as the colorimetric probe was utilized to measure the POD-like activity of CDs nanozymes in the presence of H2O2. It can be seen that only when CDs, TMB, and H2O2 coexist, a distinct absorption peak appears at 652 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2A) with the solution color changing to blue (Inset of Fig. 2A), which clarifies that CDs exhibit excellent POD-like activity and can catalyze H2O2 to produce ROS which convert TMB to blue ox-TMB. The POD-like activity of enzyme is influenced by various factors, which primarily include enzyme concentration, pH value, reaction temperature and reaction time in the catalytic system [43]. Fig. S6A-D demonstrated that the POD-like activity of CDs nanozyme reach the highest at a CDs concentration of 0.16 mg mL−1, pH 4.0, and a reaction time of 15 min at 40 °C. Therefore, subsequent experiments were conducted under the above optimal conditions. Additionally, CDs still maintained high POD-like activity even stored at 4 °C after 30 days (Fig. 2B) or dispersed at high salt concentrations (Fig. 2C), implying that CDs have excellent catalytic stability, which is beneficial for further application.

The steady-state kinetic analyses were conducted using TMB and H2O2 at different concentrations as substrates to systematically evaluate the POD-like activity of CDs-nanozyme under optimal conditions. Fig. 2D-E indicated that the catalytic reactions of both TMB and H2O2 as substrates conform to the standard Michaelis-Menten model. The Michaelis constants (K m) corresponding to TMB and H2O2 were obtained by fitting Burk double reciprocal curve with values of 0.237 mM and 0.785 mM, and the maximum initial velocity (V max) were 4.92 × 10−8 M s−1 and 23.74 × 10−8 M s−1, respectively. K m represents the affinity index of the enzyme toward the corresponding substrate, and the lower the K m value, the higher the affinity [44]. Compared with the natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP) using TMB as substrate, CDs had a lower K m than that of HRP (0.434 mM) [45], and the K m of CDs using H2O2 as substrate is nearly 5 times lower than that of HRP (3.70 mM) [45], suggesting that CDs possess strong affinity for both TMB and H2O2. Meanwhile, the V max of CDs with TMB is slightly lower than that of HRP (10.0 × 10−8 M s−1) [45], but the V max of CDs with H2O2 is much higher than that of HRP (8.71 × 10−8 M s−1) [45], proving that CDs-nanozyme has a higher catalytic reaction rate. Moreover, compared with most other reported POD-like mimics, our proposed CDs-nanozyme holds better affinities and higher reaction rates for TMB and H2O2 (Table 1 ), demonstrating that CDs as a novel nanozyme exhibit excellent POD-like activity.

The CDs@GOx can catalyze glucose oxidation and produce gluconic acid and H2O2 through enzymatic reaction, which further promoted self-sufficiency of H2O2 for POD-like activity of CDs-nanozyme. Then the CDs catalyze H2O2 to ROS for enhancing ROS accumulation in tumor for efficient cancer treatment. Firstly, the formation of downstream products was analyzed in the presence of glucose. The concentration of H2O2 was quantified using titanium sulfate (Ti(SO4)2). Ti(SO4)2 reacted with H2O2 to generate H2TiO4 (Ti4+ + 2H2O2 → H2TiO4 + 4H+) with a characteristic absorption at 405 nm [46]. As seen in Fig. 2F and Fig. S7AB, the absorbance at 405 nm increased with the increase of glucose concentration after the addition of CDs@GOx to the glucose solution, indicating that CDs@GOx in glucose solution can generate H2O2, whereas the CDs cannot yield H2O2 due to the lack of GOx.

Likewise, another downstream product, gluconic acid, was also analyzed through the formation of hydroxamate complex. In brief, gluconic acid selectively reacts with hydroxylamine to form an acid-hydroxamate complex, which then generates hydroxamate-Fe3+ complex (gluconic acid + hydroxylamine + Fe3+ → hydroxamate-Fe3+) with a typical absorbance at 505 nm [46]. Compared with CDs, the absorption of CDs@GOx solution at 505 nm was enhanced with the increase of glucose levels (Fig. 2G and Fig.S7C-D), indicating that CDs@GOx can accelerate glucose decomposition to produce gluconic acid. Similarly, in the presence of CDs@GOx, the solution exhibits obvious decrease in pH value (Fig. 2H), further confirming the generation of gluconic acid [28]. Due to the absence of GOx, CDs do not generate a large amount of H2O2 and gluconic acid, resulting in steady pH reading. These findings revealed remarkable enzymatic activity of CDs@GOx nanocatalysts simulates glucose deprivation for starvation therapy of tumors. Additionally, the ROS producing capability of CDs@GOx was performed by introducing various doses of glucose. The absorbance spectra of ox-TMB at 652 nm were more pronounced as glucose increases from 0 to 1.0 mM (Fig. S8), proving the activation of ROS by self-supplying H2O2 via glucose metabolism. Michaelis-Menten curve (Fig. 2I) demonstrated the excellent catalyzing efficiency with V max of l.43 × 10−8 M s−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic mechanism of CDs and CDs@GOx based nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: The type of ROS generated during the catalytic reaction process was evaluated to investigate the catalytic mechanism of POD-like activity of CDs and CDs@GOx based nanozyme. The isopropanol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (PBQ) and sodium azide (NaN3) were used as scavengers for hydroxyl radical (•OH), superoxide radical anion (•O2 −), and singlet oxygen (1O2), respectively. Compared with the blank group (the absence of ROS scavenger), the POD-like activity of CDs showed a slight decrease after the addition of IPA, while it significantly decreased with the introduced of PBQ and NaN3 (Fig. 3A), indicating that the ROS produced during the catalytic oxidation of H2O2 by CDs nanozymes were mainly •O2 − and 1O2, accompanied by a small amount of •OH. This conclusion is further supported by electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra. It can be observed from Fig. 3B-D that there are enhanced ROS (•OH, •O2 −, and 1O2) signals when CDs nanozymes, H2O2 and trapping agents (DMPO or TEMP) coexist compared to only CDs nanozymes or H2O2 and capture agents exist, and the •O2 − (1:1:1:1, the two peaks in the middle split into distinct small peaks) and 1O2 (1:1:1) signals are obviously stronger than the •OH (1:2:2:1) signal. In addition, the 1O2 generated was also detected using a SOSG probe possessing faint blue fluorescence itself, while a bright green fluorescence is generated once the probe encounters 1O2 instead of other ROS. As shown in Fig. 3E, the results uncovered that the fluorescence of SOSG for CDs + H2O2 was enhanced compared to free CDs and H2O2 alone, implying an increase generation of 1O2 when CDs and H2O2 coexist. Importantly, the CDs@GOx displayed stronger ROS signals, proving the more ROS production owing to the presence of GOx.

According to above results, we speculate that the possible catalytic mechanism of CDs nanozymes is as Fig. 3F. The surface of CDs contains abundant carboxyl groups (−COOH), which can assist in the decomposition of H2O2. On the one hand, the −COOH of CDs is oxidized by H2O2 to form peroxycarboxylic acid (−COOOH), which then undergoes homolytic fission and generates hydroxyl radicals (•OH) [41].

On the other hand, CDs nanozymes are oxidized in the presence of H2O2 to generate peroxide radicals (ROO•), which spontaneously recombine through the Russell mechanism, effectively generating 1O2 [50,51]. In addition, •O2 − is generated during the catalytic decomposition of H2O2 by CDs nanozymes. Therefore, CDs can produce various ROS and exhibit excellent POD-like activity in the presence of H2O2.

Due to the catalytic oxidation of TMB to ox-TMB, the color of CDs/TMB/H2O2 solution turns blue. Interestingly, after adding NO2 −, the interactions between CDs/TMB/H2O2 system and NO2 − resulted in an enhanced UV–vis absorption at 446 nm along with the corresponding solution color changes from blue to green (Fig. 4A-B). In contrast, the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S9) of CDs + NO2 − system shows no signal, indicating that there is no direct reaction between CDs and NO2 −. It is speculated that ox-TMB undergoes diazotization reaction with NO2 − under acidic condition (pH = 4) [27]. Specifically, CDs catalyze H2O2 to produce ROS and oxidize colorless TMB to generate blue intermediate ox-TMB. When inducing GSH, ox-TMB can be completely reduced to colorless TMB. However, when NO2 − is present, ox-TMB intermediate reacts with NO2 − to generate green diazotized ox-TMB, where GSH can only reduce diazotized ox-TMB to yellow diazotized TMB, not to colorless TMB, which owing to the fact that GSH can reduce semiquinone imine structure of diazotized ox-TMB to amino structure, but cannot reduce the diazo group (Fig. 4C). The corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra and solution color change also show the same change (Fig. 4A-B). Above results shed light on that NO2 − undergoes diazotization reaction with ox-TMB.

Apart from the visible color change of solution, the fluorescence of CDs is quenched when NO2 − exists in CDs/TMB/H2O2 system (Fig. 4D). However, this phenomenon does not appear in CDs/TMB/H2O2 or CDs + NO2 − systems (Fig. 4D). The fluorescence quenching of CDs might be related to the formation of diazotized ox-TMB [27]. The UV–vis spectra of diazotized ox-TMB and fluorescence spectrum of CDs were measured to understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism. Fig. 4E shows obvious overlapping between emission spectrum of CDs under 360 nm excitation and absorption band of diazotized ox-TMB, indicating that the emission light of CDs can be absorbed by diazotized ox-TMB. Besides, the fluorescence lifetimes before and after the addition of NO2 − were 5.40 and 5.21 ns, respectively, which remained almost unchanged (Fig. S10). So, diazotized ox-TMB can quench the fluorescence of CDs by the inner filter effect. Therefore, the simultaneous changes in visual color and fluorescence provide a feasible method for developing a ratiometric colorimetric/fluorometric platform for recognition of NO2 −.

Based on the above research results, the ratiometric colorimetric mode was first utilized to determine NO2 −. As shown in Fig. 4F, with the increase of NO2 − concentration, the UV–vis absorption of CDs/TMB/H2O2 at 652 nm is almost unchanged, while at 446 nm increases gradually. Fig. 4G exhibits a fine linear relationship between ln (A652/A446 −0.5115) and NO2 − concentrations in the range of 5–200 μM (R2 = 0.9986) with the limit of detection (LOD) of 1.01 μM. Subsequently, the NO2 − was detected using the fluorometric mode under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4H, the fluorescence of CDs/TMB/H2O2 system at 460 nm was gradually quenched with the increase of NO2 − concentration. The detection of NO2 − in aqueous solutions have been successfully achieved based on the fluorescence change of CDs/TMB/H2O2 system at 460 nm with linear range of 10–150 μM, as well as the LOD of 0.32 μM (Fig. 4I). Interestingly, owing to the obvious color changes of the solution in the reaction, the intelligent image analysis has been established for visual detection of NO2 −. Fig. 4J illustrated the process of detecting NO2 − based on the intelligent image analysis. The color of CDs/TMB/H2O2 solution changes incrementally from blue to green with increasing NO2 − concentration from 0 μM to 300 μM (Fig. 4K). The solution colors could be transformed into RGB values through a smartphone color recognition application, of which the ratio of G value and B value (G/B) could be used as a parameter for NO2 − quantification. Fig. 4L shows a satisfactory linear relationship between G/B values and NO2 − concentrations in the range of 0–300 μM with LOD of 8.22 μM. More importantly, Fig. S11 indicated that the established triple-mode method has high selectivity for detecting NO2 −, which mainly attributed to the TMB chromogenic reaction catalyzed by POD-like nanozyme and the specific diazotization interaction between ox-TMB and NO2 −.

Compared with other reported NO2 − detection strategies, the proposed triple mode of ratiometric colorimetric/fluorometric/intelligent image analysis method for recognition of NO2 − has the advantages of wide linear range and low LOD (Table 2 ). Importantly, the ratiometric colorimetric/intelligent image analysis method provided by our strategy can directly detect NO2 − with naked eyes, while fluorometric method can avoid potential color interference from the substrate, the results of three modes can self-corrected and mutually verified, effectively improving detection accuracy and reliability. To evaluate the feasibility of CDs-nanozyme in actual samples, the triple-mode methods were used to detect NO2 − in three different brands of ham sausage without going through the tedious process of defatting and deproteinization (Table S1). The test results of all three samples are close to the detection values of the national standard method, and all are below the national standard limit (≤ 30 mg/kg, GB 2760–2021). The established triple-mode NO2 − detection system can be applied in wide testing environment with the advantages of good analytical performance and simple operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anticancer effect in vitro of CDs and CDs@GOx
ce:section.ce:para: After entering cells through endocytosis, CDs reached lysosomes with weak acidity (Fig. 5A), which is beneficial for their POD-like activity [14]. The classical MTT experiments on the human liver cells (HL-7702) as normal cells and human cervical cancer cells (HeLa) were firstly conducted to verify the in vitro cytotoxicity of CDs and CDs@GOx. Compared with HL-7702 cells, the survival rate of HeLa cells significantly decreases with the increase of CDs concentration. When the concentration of CDs reached 3.0 mg mL−1, HL-7702 cells still maintain nearly 70 % viability, while the survival rate of HeLa cells is only 25 % (Fig. 5B), indicating that CDs possess high selective killing to cancer cells rather than normal cells. After loading GOx, the inhibitory effect of CDs@GOx on cancer cells is much greater than that of CDs, and the cell survival rate is only 14 % at a concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1 in glucose-free media (Fig. 5C), while the survival rate of normal cells is higher (Fig. S12), indicating that cells may begin self-reinforcing starvation attributed to glucose deprivation in cell metabolism and catalytic therapy. The tumor-inhibiting effect was dramatically enhanced in glucose media (Fig. 5C). The killing effect of CDs on cancer cells is mainly due to their high POD-like activity, which catalyzes the generation of ROS from endogenous H2O2. The endogenous GSH within tumor cells cleaned up some ROS (Fig. S13) for CDs and CDs@GOx, but this does not affect the killing ability against tumors due to accumulation of more ROS in cells and the starvation-assisted cancer treatment, which achieved non-invasive synergistic treatment for malignant tumors.

In addition, obvious green fluorescence was observed in HeLa cells incubated with CDs or CDs@GOx using DCFH-DA as a ROS fluorescent probe, but almost no ROS signal was observed in normal HL-7702 cells and untreated HeLa cells (Fig. 5D). This further suggests that ROS are generated solely in cancer cells with CDs or CDs@GOx, fully reflecting that they have selective killing effect on tumor cells instead of normal cells. Importantly, the HeLa cells-treated by CDs@GOx exhibited three times stronger ROS signal than CDs (Fig. 5E), revealing that CDs@GOx consumed glucose to produce a large amount of H2O2 for POD-like reaction to generate more ROS. Finally, the cytotoxicity and biosafety of CDs and CDs@GOx were further confirmed using Calcein-AM/Propidium Iodide (PI) staining. HeLa cells treated with CDs@GOx and high concentration CDs exhibited strong red fluorescence (Fig. 5F), which represents dead cells stained to red by PI, while untreated HeLa cells and HL-7702 normal cells still showed green fluorescence (Fig. 5F), because of live cells being stained green with Calcein-AM. The live/dead assay results further ensured the specific killing ability of CDs and CDs@GOx toward cancer cells, which is consistent with the results of MTT experiments. These results supported that CDs nanozymes can act as ROS therapeutic agents to inhibit the growth of cancer cells. The loading of GOx realized combination therapy of the starvation plus ROS for cancer treatment. The findings demonstrated that CDs and CDs@GOx can generate strong ROS to treat tumor death, which has the advantage of being non-invasive without considering external stimuli, such as the need to match the light source and it penetrate depth of the tumor during photodynamic or photothermal therapy, or the weak CDT efficacy alone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anticancer efficacy in vivo of CDs and CDs@GOx
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the synergistic therapeutic effect in vitro, the in vivo cancer therapeutic efficacy of CDs and CDs@GOx on the 4T1 tumor-bearing mice by intratumoral injection were assessed (Fig. 6A). As shown in Fig. 6B and Fig. S14, the tumor volume of mice treated with saline significantly increased during treatment, indicating its ineffective inhibition of tumor growth. In contrast, mice injected with CDs exhibited a detectable slowdown in tumor growth, with a tumor volume about one-third that of saline treated mice. In sharp contrast, CDs@GOx enhanced tumor suppression in 4T1 tumor-bearing mice, with a tumor volume approximately one-quarter that of saline treated mice. Due to the modification of GOx, CDs@GOx catalyze overexpressed glucose in tumor cells to produce more H2O2, which further amplified the production of ROS and enhanced tumor inhibition efficacy. The tumor suppression efficiency is comparable to reported tumor treatment effects achieved by various carrier-loaded GOx systems [55–57]. Fig. 6C depicted the average weights of relevant anatomical tumors after different treatments. The results are consistent with Fig. 6B, proving that CDs and CDs@GOx have an effective in vivo anti-tumor effect, and the effect of CDs@GOx is superior to that of CDs. Furthermore, no significant change in the body weight of mice (Fig. 6D), and there were no obvious differences in the organ indices of heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney between the mice-treated by CDs or CDs@GOx compared with the control group without transplanted tumors (Fig. 6E), indicating that the CDs or CDs@GOx had no noticeable toxic side effects. Nevertheless, the liver and spleen indices in the saline group increased significantly (Fig. 6E), which may be due to the destruction of the immune system caused by excessive tumor volume [58].

Subsequently, the biocompatibility of CDs and CDs@GOx was evaluated through the hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of main organs in mice from different treatment groups. As shown in Fig. 6F, no significant tissue damage or pathological changes were observed in hearts and kidneys of mice in each group.

Except for the control group (healthy mice without tumors), all other groups of mice exhibited mild acute and chronic inflammation in the livers and lungs, and the spleens produced extramedullary hematopoiesis, which may be due to the tumor-bearing affects the normal physiological function of the mice, leading to myelosuppression of spleens and inflammation of livers and lungs. However, the extramedullary hematopoiesis in spleens of mice in CDs and CDs@GOx groups were significantly weaker than that in saline group. More interestingly, all tumor slices in each group showed significant necrosis, and there exist the large blood vessels nourishing the tumor in saline group (The black arrow indicated in Fig. 6F), while no similar large blood vessels were found in the other two groups, indicating that the large blood vessels were destroyed and significantly inhibited tumor growth for CDs and CDs@GOx groups. On the contrary, the mice-treated by saline group had obviously larger tumor volume compared to both the CDs and CDs@GOx groups. The results revealed that CDs and CDs@GOx possess good biocompatibility and show strong potential prospects for cancer treatment.

Summary, a new kind of metal-free CDs-based nanozymes with POD-like activity was established by a one-step solvothermal method using spinach powder as carbon source. The as-prepared CDs exhibited excellent photostability, high catalytic activity, and good biocompatibility. Due to the solution color and fluorescence changes caused by NO2 −, the triple ratiometric colorimetric, fluorometric and smartphone assisted recognition of NO2 − has been achieved. Taking advantages of high POD-like catalytic activity, the CDs have been successfully used as therapeutic agents to against cancer by catalyze the overexpression of H2O2 in cancer cells to produce highly toxic ROS instead of normal cells. Besides, CDs@GOx nanocatalysts integrate CDs with GOx in one platform, which not only consumed the glucose energy supply that tumors rely on for survival, but also generate H2O2 that is further catalyzed by CDs-nanozymes to produce more ROS to kill tumors more efficiently, thus developing traditional tumor starvation therapy into a more efficient tumor “starvation” therapy. This work not only developed a novel and efficient CDs nanozyme with POD-like activity for multimodal chemical sensing, but also utilized the catalytic properties of CDs and GOx to expand their application potential in cancer therapy. However, considering the toxicity associated with GOx exposure for CDs@GOx, encapsulation techniques involving hydrophobic proteins or hyaluronic acid can be employed in future experiments. This approach not only mitigats physiological toxicity but also substantially enhances the tumor-targeting ability, thereby further improving the efficacy of tumor treatment. In addition, the relatively short excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs and CDs@GOx render them inappropriate for in vivo fluorescence imaging. Thus, to address this limitation, composite nanoparticles can be fabricated by incorporating nano-MnO2. Utilizing the magnetic resonance imaging properties of Mn2+ that is generated via the series of in-vivo reactions of MnO₂, these composite nanoparticles facilitate integrated diagnosis and treatment.
